learningGoal: How to calculate distance using speed and time
Why distance equals speed multiplied by time

screens.title: How far did Priya go?
screens.body:
Priya cycled at a steady speed from her house to the beach.
They travelled at **12 km/h** for **3 hours**.
We know the speed and the time — but how **far** did they go? There's a simple way to work it out!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Priya travels at **15 km/h** for **4 hours**. How far do they go?

interaction.question: What is 15 × 4?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Distance = Speed × Time = 15 × 4 = **60 km**.

learningGoal: How to apply the distance rule to real journeys and races
When to watch out for using the wrong units in distance calculations

screens.title: How far at 50 mph?
screens.body:
Sophie is on a road trip and wondering how far the family has driven.
They've been going at **50 mph** for **3 hours**.
Can you figure out the distance without a sat-nav? All you need is a **one-step multiplication**!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Travelling at **40 mph** for **4 hours**. What's the distance?

interaction.question: What is 40 × 4?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! **160 miles**. Just multiply and you're done! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 40 × 4 = **160 miles**. Distance = Speed × Time.

learningGoal: How to calculate time using distance and speed
Why time equals distance divided by speed

screens.title: How long will it take?
screens.body:
Ben is driving from London to Brighton at a steady speed.
The journey is **120 miles** and they travel at **60 mph**.
We know how far and how fast — but how **long** will it take?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Ben travels **150 miles** at **50 mph**. How long does it take?

interaction.question: What is 150 ÷ 50?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Time = Distance ÷ Speed = 150 ÷ 50 = **3 hours**.

learningGoal: How to avoid dividing speed by distance instead of the other way round
When to watch out for giving a decimal answer when minutes are needed

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A journey of **180 miles** at **60 mph**. How long does it take?

interaction.question: What is 180 ÷ 60?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 180 ÷ 60 = **3 hours**. Remember: for time, always DIVIDE! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Time = Distance ÷ Speed = 180 ÷ 60 = **3 hours**.

learningGoal: How to use the speed-distance-time triangle
How to cover up the value you want to find

screens.title: Cover up what you want to find!
Your turn — use the triangle!
screens.body: Now you need to find the **distance**. Which letter do you cover up?

interaction.question: To find distance, which letter do you cover?
interaction.correctFeedback: That's right! Cover **D** and you see **S × T**. So Distance = Speed × Time! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! To find distance, cover up **D**. What's left is **S × T** — so Distance = Speed × Time.

screens.title: The SDT triangle — your secret weapon!

learningGoal: How to apply the SDT (Speed, Distance, Time) triangle to quickly choose the right rule
When to watch out for covering up the wrong letter in the triangle

screens.title: Find the distance!
screens.body: Speed = **40 mph** and Time = **3 hours**. Cover **D** — what formula do you get?

interaction.question: What is the distance?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! Distance = Speed × Time = **120 miles**. The triangle never lies! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Distance = Speed × Time. Work it out: the answer is **120 miles**.

subConcepts.name: Converting Minutes to Hours for SDT

learningGoal: How to convert (change) minutes into hours for speed calculations
Why 30 minutes is 0.5 hours, not 0.30 hours

screens.title: 30 minutes — but the speed is per HOUR!
Divide minutes by 60!
screens.body:
There are **60 minutes** in an hour. So to convert minutes to hours, divide by 60:

**30 ÷ 60 = 0.5 hours**

**Key conversions to memorise:**
15 min = 0.25 hrs (¼) | 30 min = 0.5 hrs (½) | 45 min = 0.75 hrs (¾) | 20 min = ⅓ hr ≈ 0.333

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Convert **45 minutes** into hours. Remember: divide by 60!

interaction.question: 45 minutes = ? hours
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! 45 ÷ 60 = **0.75 hours**. Always divide by 60, never just move the decimal! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 45 ÷ 60 = **0.75 hours**. Remember: 60 minutes = 1 hour, so divide by 60.

learningGoal: How to avoid the 0.30 hours trap when converting 30 minutes
When to watch out for treating minutes as decimals of an hour

screens.title: Minutes are NOT decimals of an hour!
screens.body:
Ben wrote 30 minutes as 0.30 hours instead of 0.5 hours.

**30 minutes is NOT 0.3 hours!**
To convert: divide by 60 (because there are 60 minutes in an hour).

30 ÷ 60 = **0.5 hours**

steps.why: Just moving the decimal point doesn't work!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is the correct speed for 9 km in 45 minutes?

interaction.question: What is 9 ÷ 0.75?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 9 ÷ 0.75 = **12 km/h**. You converted the minutes properly! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! First convert: 45 min = 0.75 hours. Then 9 ÷ 0.75 = **12 km/h**.

subConcepts.name: Converting Metres to Kilometres for SDT

learningGoal: How to convert (change) metres to kilometres
When to change the units before calculating speed

screens.title: 1000 metres = 1 kilometre!
screens.body:
The key fact:
**1 km = 1000 m**

To convert metres to km: **divide by 1000**
To convert km to metres: **multiply by 1000**

1500 m ÷ 1000 = **1.5 km**

steps.why: Divide by 1000 to go from m to km

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Convert **2500 metres** to kilometres.

interaction.question: 2500 metres = ? km
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 2500 ÷ 1000 = **2.5 km**. Divide by 1000 every time! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 2500 ÷ 1000 = **2.5 km**. Remember: 1000 metres = 1 kilometre.

learningGoal: How to avoid dividing by 100 instead of 1000 when converting metres to km
When to watch out for mixing metres and kilometres in the same calculation

screens.title: Convert metres to km FIRST!
screens.body:
Daisy used metres instead of km: 1500 ÷ 0.5 = 3000 km/h — impossible!

The speed is in **km/h**, so the distance must be in **km** too!

1500 m ÷ 1000 = **1.5 km**
Then: 1.5 ÷ 0.5 = **3 km/h** — much more sensible!

steps.why: Used metres with km/h — units don't match!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Someone ran **2000 m** in **0.5 hours**. What is the correct speed in km/h?

interaction.question: What is 2 ÷ 0.5?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 2 ÷ 0.5 = **4 km/h**. Always convert metres to km first! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Convert first: 2000 m = 2 km. Then 2 ÷ 0.5 = **4 km/h**.

learningGoal: How to convert (change) km/h to m/s
Why you multiply by 1000 then divide by 3600

screens.title: 72 km/h — but what's that in m/s?
Your turn!
screens.body: Convert **54 km/h** to m/s. Use the shortcut: divide by 3.6!

interaction.question: What is 54 ÷ 3.6?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 54 ÷ 3.6 = **15 m/s**. The ÷ 3.6 trick works every time! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 54 ÷ 3.6 = **15 m/s**. Remember: km/h to m/s = divide by 3.6.

learningGoal: How to avoid multiplying or dividing in the wrong order when converting speed units
When to watch out for confusing km/h to m/s with m/s to km/h

screens.title: You need BOTH conversions!
screens.body:
Tom converted km to metres but forgot to convert hours to seconds.

To go from km/h to m/s, you need **two** steps:
1. km → m (× 1000)
2. hours → seconds (÷ 3600)

Or use the shortcut: **÷ 3.6**

steps.text: WRONG: just multiplied by 1000 and forgot to divide by 3600
Check: a fast car ≈ 30 m/s, sprinter ≈ 10 m/s

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Convert **72 km/h** to m/s correctly.

interaction.question: What is 72 km/h in m/s?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 72 ÷ 3.6 = **20 m/s**. Both units must be converted! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 72 ÷ 3.6 = **20 m/s**. Remember: divide by 3.6 to go from km/h to m/s.

learningGoal: How to convert (change) a decimal answer back into minutes
Why 0.25 hours equals 15 minutes

screens.title: 0.75 hours — but what's that in minutes?
screens.body:
Grace calculated her running time as 0.75 hours and needs to know the minutes.
But **0.75 hours** isn't very useful — She ran a 5K in the park.
How many **minutes** is 0.75 hours?

steps.why: We need to convert to a unit we understand!

screens.title: Multiply by 60!
screens.body:
To convert hours to minutes, **multiply by 60** (because there are 60 minutes in an hour).

**0.75 × 60 = 45 minutes**

That makes much more sense!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Convert **0.5 hours** into minutes.

interaction.question: 0.5 hours = ? minutes
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 0.5 × 60 = **30 minutes**. Always multiply by 60! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 0.5 × 60 = **30 minutes**. Multiply decimal hours by 60 to get minutes.

screens.title: Decimal hours → minutes: × 60!
screens.body: Key decimal-to-minutes conversions:

steps.why: The whole part stays, multiply the decimal by 60 ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid leaving a time answer as a confusing decimal
When to watch out for writing 0.5 hours as 50 minutes instead of 30

screens.title: 0.25 hours = 25 minutes?
screens.body:
Isaac calculated a journey time of 0.25 hours.
Isaac wrote: "0.25 hours = **25 minutes**".

That's not right! Can you spot the error?

screens.title: Multiply by 60, not 100!
screens.body:
Isaac read 0.25 hours as 25 minutes — but 0.25 means a QUARTER, not 25!

Hours are NOT like money (where 0.25 = 25p). There are **60 minutes** in an hour, not 100!

**0.25 × 60 = 15 minutes**

steps.why: Treated decimals like pence — but hours have 60 mins, not 100!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: How many minutes is **0.5 hours**?

interaction.question: 0.5 hours = ? minutes
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! 0.5 × 60 = **30 minutes**. Multiply by 60, never by 100! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 0.5 × 60 = **30 minutes**. Remember: 60 minutes in an hour.

screens.title: Hours are NOT like money!
screens.body: The big trap: treating decimal hours like pounds and pence.

learningGoal: How to calculate average speed (total distance divided by total time) for a journey with two parts
Why you must NOT just average the two speeds

screens.title: What's the average speed?
screens.body:
Aisha drove to her grandmother's house in two parts.
Part 1 (through the town): **60 miles** at **30 mph**
Part 2 (on the main road): **40 miles** at **40 mph**

What was their average speed? **Don't** just add the speeds and divide by 2!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Total distance = **80 miles**. Total time = **2 hours**.
What is the average speed?

interaction.question: What is 80 ÷ 2 (to the nearest whole number)?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 80 ÷ 2 = **40 mph**. Total distance ÷ total time! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Average speed = total distance ÷ total time = 80 ÷ 2 = **40 mph**.

screens.title: Average speed — the golden rule!
screens.body: Average speed is NOT the average of the two speeds:

steps.text: Average speed = TOTAL distance ÷ TOTAL time
steps.why: Add up all the distances, add up all the times, then divide

steps.text: DON'T add speeds and divide by 2!
steps.why: This only works if you spend the SAME time at each speed

steps.text: You may need to calculate each time first
steps.why: Time = Distance ÷ Speed for each part

steps.text: Then add distances, add times, and divide!
steps.why: That gives the true average speed ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid the trap of just averaging two speeds
Why total distance divided by total time is the only correct method

screens.title: Sophie took a shortcut — and got it wrong!
screens.body:
Sophie drove 60 miles at 30 mph, then 60 miles at 60 mph.
Sophie calculated: **(30 + 60) ÷ 2 = 45 mph**.

Is that correct? Or did Sophie fall into the biggest SDT trap?

steps.why: Just averaged the two speeds — is that OK?

screens.title: You can't just average the speeds!
screens.body:
Sophie added the two speeds and divided by 2. That's the **wrong** method!

You spend **different amounts of time** at each speed, so you must use:
**Average speed = total distance ÷ total time**

steps.why: Simply averaging speeds ignores how long you spent at each one

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Total distance = **200 miles**, total time = **4 hours**.
What is the correct average speed?

interaction.question: What is 200 ÷ 4?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 200 ÷ 4 = **50 mph**. Total distance ÷ total time! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Average speed = 200 ÷ 4 = **50 mph**. Don't just average the two speeds!

screens.title: The average speed trap!
screens.body: This is the sneakiest question type in the 11+:

steps.text: NEVER add speeds and divide by 2!
steps.why: That only works if you spend equal TIME at each speed

steps.text: Average speed = total distance ÷ total time
Work out each journey time first if needed
steps.why: Time = Distance ÷ Speed for each part

steps.text: Then: add all distances, add all times, divide!

learningGoal: How to pick out speed, distance, and time from a wordy question
How to decide which rule to use

screens.title: A wordy question — where do you start?
screens.body:
Here's the question:
"A train travels at 80 mph from London to Manchester, which is 160 miles away. How long is the journey?"

Long and confusing? **Not if you know the trick.** Start by finding the three key pieces: **speed**, **distance**, and **time**.

visualProps.label: Find S, D, and T in the question

screens.title: Pick out S, D, and T!
Your turn!
screens.body: A train travels at 80 mph from London to Manchester, which is 160 miles away. How long is the journey?

interaction.question: What is the answer?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! You picked the right rule and got **2 hours**! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Identify: Distance = 160 miles, Speed = 80 mph. Time = 160 ÷ 80 = 2 hours. The answer is **2 hours**.

screens.title: Word problems — a step-by-step recipe!
screens.body: For any SDT word problem:

steps.text: Step 1: READ the question — what are you asked to find?
Step 3: CHOOSE the right rule using SDT triangle
steps.why: Cover up what you need to find

steps.text: Step 4: CHECK — does your answer make sense?

learningGoal: How to avoid using the wrong values from a multi-step word problem
When to watch out for extra information that is not needed in the calculation

screens.title: Where did Grace go wrong?
screens.body:
A bus travels 90 miles in 1 hour 30 minutes. What is its average speed?

Grace worked it out and got **90 mph**.

That doesn't seem right. Can you spot the mistake?

steps.why: forgot 1 hr 30 min = 1.5 hours, not 1 hour. Used 90 ÷ 1 = 90 mph

screens.title: Convert the time properly!
screens.body:
Grace forgot 1 hr 30 min = 1.5 hours, not 1 hour. Used 90 ÷ 1 = 90 mph

The time must be converted to a **decimal** before dividing.

**Convert: 1 hr 30 min = 1.5 hours. Speed = 90 ÷ 1.5 = 60 mph**

**Watch out for these traps:**
1 hr 30 min = **1.5** hours (NOT 1.30!)
1 hr 15 min = **1.25** hours (NOT 1.15!)
2 hr 45 min = **2.75** hours (NOT 2.45!)
Always divide the minutes by 60, then add to the hours.

steps.text: WRONG: forgot 1 hr 30 min = 1.5 hours, not 1 hour. Used 90 ÷ 1 = 90 mph
RIGHT: Convert: 1 hr 30 min = 1.5 hours. Speed = 90 ÷ 1.5 = 60 mph

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A vehicle travels **100 miles** in **2.5 hours**. What is its speed?

interaction.question: What is 100 ÷ 2.5?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 100 ÷ 2.5 = **40 mph**. You converted the time properly! ✓

screens.title: Word problem checklist!
screens.body: Follow these steps every time you get a speed, distance or time word problem:

steps.text: Step 1: Read the question — what are you finding?
steps.why: Speed, distance, or time? Cover that letter in the triangle

steps.text: Step 2: Convert the time to hours first!
Step 3: Watch the trap — 1 hour 30 min is NOT 1.30
Step 4: Put the numbers into the formula and calculate
steps.why: Use the triangle: D = S × T, S = D ÷ T, T = D ÷ S ✓
